narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 46% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, test scores, and background details like your middle-income family situation and plans to attend full-time. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 grade decile ranking of 1st, which is notably lower than the 6th decile average among students who passed the bar, signaling to the model a potential struggle in later undergraduate performance. Counterbalancing this somewhat was your strong undergraduate GPA of 3.60, well above the 3.24 average for bar passers, which pulled the prediction in a more favorable direction by highlighting your overall academic capability. Your LSAT score of 33 also helped nudge things positively, though it fell short of the 37 average seen in successful bar takers, making it a moderate positive influence compared to your other Year 3 performance. While your full-time enrollment plans align closely with the vast majority of bar passers (over 93%), and your middle family income bracket is common among them (about 35%), these didn't outweigh the key academic signals. In essence, your lower Year 3 decile carried the heaviest weight toward the failure prediction, overshadowing strengths like your undergrad GPA and LSAT. Compared to typical accepted students, who tend to show steadier high deciles across years and stronger LSATs, your profile appeared riskier for bar success despite some standout elements. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's the model's data-driven read on bar passage patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the 37 average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or post-bac programs, and gathering letters from professors who can speak to your Year 3 resilience. You might also explore schools with holistic reviews or bar prep support tailored for full-time students like you. With targeted improvements, you can reshape this prediction and strengthen your next application. Keep pushing—many students turn these setbacks into bar-passing success stories.
